content:
# Hebrews Chapter 7 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.3 — "having been made like the Son of God" vs. "resembling the Son of God" vs. "He remains a priest forever, resembling the Son of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been made like the Son of God — he remains a priest perpetually" | aphōmoiōmenos de tō huiō tou theou menei hiereus eis to diēnekes |
| LOGOS | "resembling the Son of God he continues a priest forever" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "He remains a priest forever, resembling the Son of God" | Reorders — puts "priest forever" before "resembling" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The word order in Greek places "resembling the Son of God" before "he continues a priest forever" — the resemblance grounds the permanence. PNEUMA reverses the order, which is a minor structural change but does not alter the meaning significantly.

**Status:** Minor. All three are acceptable.

---

### v.16 — "a law of fleshly commandment" vs. "a legal requirement concerning bodily descent" vs. "the physical requirement of belonging to the tribe of Levi"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not according to a law of fleshly commandment" | ou kata nomon entolēs sarkinēs |
| LOGOS | "not on the basis of a legal requirement concerning bodily descent" | "Bodily descent" for sarkinēs |
| PNEUMA | "not by meeting the physical requirement of belonging to the tribe of Levi" | Specifies "tribe of Levi"; "physical" for sarkinēs |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. sarkinēs means "fleshly/bodily" — referring to physical lineage. LOGOS renders well with "bodily descent." PNEUMA specifies "belonging to the tribe of Levi" — adding the specific tribe where the author uses the general term. The contrast is between flesh (sarkinos) and life (zōē), not between Levi and Judah.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA over-specifies.

---

### v.25 — "save to the uttermost" vs. "once and forever, to save"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he is also able to save to the uttermost those approaching God through him" | sōzein eis to panteles dynatai |
| LOGOS | "he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he is able, once and forever, to save those who come to God through him" | "Once and forever" for eis to panteles |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. eis to panteles means "completely/to the uttermost" — it describes the completeness of the salvation, not its temporal character. PNEUMA's "once and forever" shifts from extent to time. The author's point: Jesus saves completely, not partially — the degree of salvation, not its frequency.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. PNEUMA shifts from completeness of salvation to its temporal character.

---

### v.27 — "this he did once for all when he offered up himself" vs. "But Jesus did this once for all when he offered himself as the sacrifice for the people's sins"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for this he did once for all when he offered up himself" | touto gar epoiēsen ephapax heauton anenegkas |
| LOGOS | "since he did this once for all when he offered up himself" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "But Jesus did this once for all when he offered himself as the sacrifice for the people's sins" | Adds "as the sacrifice for the people's sins" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says Jesus "offered up himself" (heauton anenegkas). PNEUMA adds "as the sacrifice for the people's sins" — an interpretive expansion. The author's compressed language is deliberate: "he offered up himself" — no qualifier, no specification. The self-offering is the point.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands the compressed self-offering language.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA over-specifies | 2 | "Tribe of Levi" (v.16), "sacrifice for the people's sins" (v.27) |
| PNEUMA shifts meaning | 1 | Temporal for completeness (v.25) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 7's most significant disagreement is v.25 — "save to the uttermost" (completeness) vs. "once and forever, to save" (temporality). The author's argument builds toward the completeness of Christ's salvation: because his priesthood is permanent, his saving power is total. PNEUMA's temporal rendering ("once and forever") echoes "once for all" (v.27) but misses the extent-based meaning of eis to panteles. The difference matters: is the emphasis on how completely Jesus saves, or on how permanently?

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
